EDCS-10000362.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Urbana and her son Gaetulus, erected by their heir Arcto and by Gaetulus' siblings.

EDCS-10001044.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Licinius Urbicus beneficiarius consularis votum libens solvit merito
EDCS-10001044.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Lucius Licinius Urbicus, a beneficiarius consularis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001044.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Licinius Urbicus, a military official.

EDCS-10001045.summary: This inscription consists of only fragmentary letters and cannot be reliably interpreted.

EDCS-10001048.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Gramonius Lucilianus signifer legionis II adiutricis votum solvit libens merito Maximo et Aeliano consulibus
EDCS-10001048.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Titus Gramonius Lucilianus, a standard-bearer of the Second Adiutrix Legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, during the consulship of Maximus and Aelianus.
EDCS-10001048.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Gramonius Lucilianus, a military standard-bearer, and provides a precise date through a consular dating formula.

EDCS-10001049.text_edition: TIus et - Pontius Dalmatius filius votum libentes merito posuerunt
EDCS-10001049.translation: Titus and Pontius Dalmatius, his son, willingly and deservedly placed this vow.
EDCS-10001049.summary: This is a votive inscription placed by Titus and his son Pontius Dalmatius, though the deity is not specified.

EDCS-10001052.text_edition: Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Dasius decurio municipii Bassianensium pontifex municipii eiusdem aedilitate functus municipio Spodentium vixit annis LXX et Aelis Licinio et Honorato et Silano et Iustillo et Ainato marito et filis karissimis Aelia Iusta viva sibi et suis posuit
EDCS-10001052.translation: To the Manes Gods. Publius Aelius Dasius, decurion of the municipality of Bassianae, pontifex of the same municipality, having served as aedile in the municipality of Spodentium, lived 70 years. And to Aelis, Licinius, and Honoratus, and Silanus, and Iustillus, and Ainatus, her husband, and her dearest children, Aelia Iusta, while living, placed this for herself and her family.
EDCS-10001052.summary: This is a complex funerary monument dedicated to the Manes Gods, commemorating Publius Aelius Dasius, a municipal official, and also placed by Aelia Iusta for herself, her husband, and her children.

EDCS-10001053.text_edition: Dis Manibus perpetuae securitati Aurelia Bittelliana sic ex provincia Italia vixit annos XX Florentino filio Aurelius Florus protector ducenarius coniugi
EDCS-10001053.translation: To the Manes Gods, for perpetual security. Aurelia Bittelliana, from the province of Italy, lived 20 years. To her son Florentinus. Aurelius Florus, a protector ducenarius, (made this) for his wife.
EDCS-10001053.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Manes Gods for Aurelia Bittelliana, who died at 20, placed by her husband Aurelius Florus, a military protector, also mentioning their son Florentinus.

EDCS-10001054.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Victorinus decurio Bassianensium votum libens solvit
EDCS-10001054.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aelius Victorinus, decurion of the Bassianenses, gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001054.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Aelius Victorinus, a decurion from Bassiana.

EDCS-10001055.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo  Aurelius Propincus sic princeps cohortis primae Campanorum votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10001055.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Propincus, thus the princeps of the first cohort of Campanians, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001055.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter by Aurelius Propincus, the princeps of the First Cohort of Campanians.

EDCS-10001057.text_edition: - Aurelius Super veteranus legionis II adiutricis missus honesta missione Apro et Maximo consulibus  votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10001057.translation: Aurelius Super, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, honorably discharged during the consulship of Aper and Maximus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001057.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Super, a discharged veteran of the Legio II Adiutrix, dating to the consulship of Aper and Maximus (207 AD).

EDCS-10001058.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Proculus decurio coloniae Cibalensium votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10001058.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aelius Proculus, decurion of the colony of Cibalae, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001058.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Aelius Proculus, a decurion of the colonia Cibalae.

EDCS-10001059.translation: Finctor, live thus for your masters.
EDCS-10001059.summary: This is a brief, personal inscription, likely a dedication or instruction to Finctor, concerning his duty to his masters.

EDCS-10001061.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Cocceius Eutychianus immunis  pro se et suis
EDCS-10001061.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Aurelius Cocceius Eutychianus, immune, for himself and his family.
EDCS-10001061.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter by Marcus Aurelius Cocceius Eutychianus, made on behalf of himself and his family, likely indicating a granted immunity from certain duties.

EDCS-10001062.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Iulianus beneficiarius consularis legionis IIII Flaviae
EDCS-10001062.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Caius Iulius Iulianus, beneficiarius consularis of the Fourth Flavian Legion.
EDCS-10001062.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Caius Iulius Iulianus, an officer (beneficiarius consularis) of the Legio IIII Flavia.

EDCS-10001063.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Fortunatus Augusti libertus
EDCS-10001063.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Fortunatus, freedman of the Emperor.
EDCS-10001063.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Fortunatus, an imperial freedman.

EDCS-10001064.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Minervae sacrum Caius Iulius Magnus Augustalis coloniae Mursensium votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10001064.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno, and Minerva, Caius Iulius Magnus, Augustalis of the colony of Mursa, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001064.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Capitoline Triad (Jupiter, Juno, Minerva) by Caius Iulius Magnus, an Augustalis from the colonia Mursa.

EDCS-10001066.text_edition: Silvano Augusto Marcus Aurelius Severus equo publico votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10001066.translation: To Silvanus Augustus, Marcus Aurelius Severus, with public horse, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001066.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus Augustus by Marcus Aurelius Severus, indicating his status as an eques publicus (one entitled to a public horse).

EDCS-10001067.text_edition: Terrae Matri Marcus Marius Secundinus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10001067.translation: To Mother Earth, Marcus Marius Secundinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-10001067.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Marius Secundinus dedicating an altar or monument to Mother Earth for himself and his household.

EDCS-10001068.text_edition: ntius Antoninus tribunus militum legionum I et II adiutricium praefectus cohortis I Ulpiae Traianae Cugernorum votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10001068.translation: [---]ntius Antoninus, military tribune of the First and Second Adiutrix legions, prefect of the First Ulpian Trajanic Cohort of the Cugerni, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10001068.summary: This is a votive inscription by a high-ranking military officer, Antoninus, who held positions in various legions and cohorts, dedicating a monument to an unspecified deity.

EDCS-10001073.translation: To our lord Cl[---], may you live and enjoy good fortune!
EDCS-10001073.summary: This is a personal message or dedication, possibly on a funerary monument or an object, wishing good fortune to someone named Cl[---].

EDCS-10102009.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Iulius Serenus veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae felicis sarcophagum condidit et vivus posuit nnis LXXX mensibus  diebus V sepultas Iulias Heracliam et Sabinillam filias et Aureliam Reginam uxorem
EDCS-10102009.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Iulius Serenus, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Happy Legion, had this sarcophagus made and placed it while living. Buried within are his daughters Iulia Heraclia and Sabinilla, and his wife Aurelia Regina, having lived 80 years, [---] months, and 5 days.
EDCS-10102009.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a sarcophagus, commissioned by a veteran, Gaius Iulius Serenus, for himself and his deceased wife and daughters.

EDCS-10102010.translation: Cornutus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10102010.summary: This is a brief votive inscription by Cornutus, dedicating a monument to an unspecified deity.

EDCS-10102012.text_edition: III Aurelius Inteulus vixit nnos IIII Aurelius Vitalianus filibus sic bene merentibus hic siti sunt
EDCS-10102012.translation: Aurelius Inteulus lived for 4 years. Aurelius Vitalianus [and his] sons, thus well-deserving, are buried here.
EDCS-10102012.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Inteulus, who died at a young age, and Aurelius Vitalianus and his sons, likely marking a family burial plot.

EDCS-10102013.text_edition: Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Quintinus miles cohortis
EDCS-10102013.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Quintinus, soldier of the cohort [---].
EDCS-10102013.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Quintinus, a soldier, likely incomplete or part of a larger monument.

EDCS-10102015.text_edition: Sen nnos XV hic sit-- est  Lic
EDCS-10102015.translation: Sen[---] lived 15 years. Here lies [---]. Lic[---].
EDCS-10102015.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for someone who lived 15 years, with other names partially preserved.

EDCS-10102017.text_edition: Ager vici Iosista adsignatus Tiberio Claudio Prisco praefecto alae I civium Romanorum  caput agri excepti
EDCS-10102017.translation: The land of the village of Iosista was assigned to Tiberius Claudius Priscus, prefect of the First Ala of Roman Citizens. The headland of the exempted land.
EDCS-10102017.summary: This inscription records the official assignment of land in the village of Iosista to Tiberius Claudius Priscus, a military officer, likely detailing a land grant or property allocation.

EDCS-10102018.text_edition: Libero Patri sacrum Caius Antonius Sabinus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10102018.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater. Gaius Antonius Sabinus, a consular beneficiarius, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10102018.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Antonius Sabinus, a consular beneficiarius (an auxiliary soldier attached to a governor's staff), dedicating a monument to the god Liber Pater.

EDCS-10102019.text_edition: Invicto deo Mithrae Veturius Dubitatus veteranus alae I civium Romanorum ex duplicario  votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10102019.translation: To the unconquered god Mithras, Veturius Dubitatus, a veteran of the First Wing of Roman Citizens, formerly a duplicarius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10102019.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mithras by Veturius Dubitatus, a veteran and former duplicarius of a Roman cavalry unit.

EDCS-10102020.text_edition: Aurelio De equiti alae armorum cohortis?  vixit annos stipendiorum X heres ex testamento faciendum curavit
EDCS-10102020.translation: To Aurelius De..., a cavalryman of the ala armorum cohort..., he lived for X years, having served X stipends. His heir, according to his will, had this made.
EDCS-10102020.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Aurelius De..., a cavalryman, erected by his heir as per his will, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-10102021.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Quintus Sabinius Maximus miles legionis I adiutricis centuria Egnati Paeti solvit libens merito
EDCS-10102021.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Quintus Sabinius Maximus, soldier of the First Legion Adiutrix, from the century of Egnatius Paetus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10102021.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Quintus Sabinius Maximus, a soldier of Legion I Adiutrix.

EDCS-10102022.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Valerius Florentinus veteranus
EDCS-10102022.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Valerius Florentinus, veteran.
EDCS-10102022.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Valerius Florentinus, a veteran.

EDCS-10102024.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelia AsclIepiodota#Asclepiodota#ASCIEPIODOTA vixit annos XIIII Aurelius Alexsander sic sorori carissimae posuit c p f
EDCS-10102024.translation: To the Divine Manes, Aurelia Asclepiodota lived 14 years. Aurelius Alexander thus placed this for his dearest sister, and had it made at his own expense.
EDCS-10102024.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated to Aurelia Asclepiodota, who lived 14 years, placed by her brother Aurelius Alexander.

EDCS-10102025.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a larger dedication or record, with a possible reference to Bononia.

EDCS-10102034.text_edition: A In pace ω innocenti Timotheo Timotheus et parentes filio
EDCS-10102034.translation: In peace, to the innocent Timotheus. Timotheus and his parents (made this) for their son.
EDCS-10102034.summary: This inscription is a Christian funerary monument dedicated to Timotheus by his parents and another Timotheus, likely an infant or young child given the term 'innocent'.

EDCS-10102070.translation: ...to his wife, Publius Tadius... placed this.
EDCS-10102070.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a funerary dedication to a wife, placed by Publius Tadius.

EDCS-10102083.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription, preserving only parts of two names, Asterius and Cleca.

EDCS-10102093.translation: To the Eternal God, Publius Aelius Eutyches (made this).
EDCS-10102093.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Eternal God by Publius Aelius Eutyches, likely reflecting a monotheistic belief, possibly Christian or Jewish.

EDCS-10102094.text_edition: Herculi Augusto Caius Valerius Mucianus pro salute sua suorumque omnium votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10102094.translation: To Hercules Augustus, Gaius Valerius Mucianus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his own safety and that of all his kin.
EDCS-10102094.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Hercules Augustus by Gaius Valerius Mucianus, made for the well-being of himself and his family.

EDCS-10102095.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Titi Flavi Martini decurionis coloniae Mursensium praefecti collegii centonariorum IIviri designati Flavius Philippus libertus  votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10102095.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Flavius Philippus, a freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the safety of Titus Flavius Martinus, decurion of the colony of Mursia, prefect of the guild of felt-makers, and designated duumvir.
EDCS-10102095.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the freedman Flavius Philippus for the health and safety of Titus Flavius Martinus, a notable citizen of Mursia.

EDCS-10102096.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Messius votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10102096.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Messius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10102096.summary: This inscription is a simple votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Messius.

EDCS-10102097.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Minervae sacrum Caius Iulius nus
EDCS-10102097.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, Minerva, Gaius Julius nus.
EDCS-10102097.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, and Minerva, with the dedicant's name partially preserved as Gaius Julius.

EDCS-10102098.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Magnus, Gaius nius.
EDCS-10102098.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Magnus by a person named Gaius, whose family name is partially lost.

EDCS-10102100.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Cai filio Claudia Verecundo Iuvavo militi legionis X geminae piae fidelis centuria Paeti stipendiorum XX heres faciendum curavit
EDCS-10102100.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, for Gaius Julius Verecundus, son of Gaius, of the Claudia tribe, from Iuvavum, a soldier of the Tenth Legion Gemina Pia Fidelis, in the centuria of Paetus, with twenty years of service, his heir had this made.
EDCS-10102100.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument erected by the heir of Gaius Julius Verecundus, a soldier of the Legio X Gemina, who died after twenty years of service.

EDCS-10102101.text_edition: Velagenus Manti filius Ulattius eques cohortis II Alpinorum nnorum XXXVIII stipendiorum XVI hic situs est Longinus |(centurio) et Primus custos armorum beneficiarius heredes posuerunt
EDCS-10102101.translation: Velagenus Ulattius, son of Mantus, a cavalryman of the Second Alpine Cohort, 38 years old, with 16 years of service, is buried here. Longinus, the centurion, and Primus, the armorer and beneficiarius, his heirs, set this up.
EDCS-10102101.summary: This inscription is a tombstone for Velagenus Ulattius, a cavalryman of the Cohors II Alpinorum, erected by his heirs, a centurion and an armorer, detailing his military service and age.

EDCS-10102102.text_edition: Dis Manibus  ValeIrio#Valerio#VALIRIO Martiali ex subaedianis collegae lapidari posuerunt
EDCS-10102102.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, for Valerius Martialis, from the subaedian camp, his fellow stonemasons erected this.
EDCS-10102102.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to Valerius Martialis, set up by his colleagues, who were stonemasons from the subaedian camp.

EDCS-10102104.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Fulguratori Caius Aelius Surinus decurio coloniae Mursensium IIviralis quinquennalis designatus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10102104.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus the Thunderer, Gaius Aelius Surinus, decurion of the colony of Mursia, designated quinquennial duumvir, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10102104.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Fulgurator by Gaius Aelius Surinus, a prominent municipal official in Mursia.

EDCS-10200169.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator VI pater patriae consul III veteranis qui militaverunt in classe Misenensi sub Sexto Lucilio Basso qui sena et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant et sunt deducti Paestum quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem V Idus Februarias Imperatore Caesare Vespasiano Augusto III Marco Cocceio Nerva consulibus |(centurioni) Liccaio Birsi filio Marsunnia loco XXIII descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio in podio arae gentis Iuliae  Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator VI pater patriae consul III veteranis qui militaverunt in classe Misenensi sub Sexto Lucilio Basso qui sena et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant et sunt deducti Paestum quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem V Idus Februarias Imperatore Caesare Vespasiano Augusto III Marco Cocceio Nerva consulibus |(centurioni) Liccaius sic Birsi filio Marsunnia loco XXIII descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio in podio arae gentis Iuliae  Marci Viri Marcelli decurionis legionis Savariensis Luci Domiti Severi veterani Breuci Cai Marci Nobilis Emonensis Cai Pidieni Aquileiensis Luci Valeri Pauonis veterani Breucio Cai Iuli Clari Aquileiensis Luci Minei Iucundi Aquileiensis
EDCS-10200169.translation: Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, in his second tribunician power, acclaimed imperator six times, father of his country, consul three times, granted citizenship to veterans who served in the Misenum fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who had completed twenty-six or more years of service and were settled at Paestum, to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they later married, provided only one wife per man, before the fifth day before the Ides of February, when Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus was consul for the third time and Marcus Cocceius Nerva was consul. For Centurion Liccaius Marsunnia, son of Birso, described and verified in the twenty-third place from the bronze tablet affixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the podium of the altar of the Julian gens. Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, in his second tribunician power, acclaimed imperator six times, father of his country, consul three times, granted citizenship to veterans who served in the Misenum fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who had completed twenty-six or more years of service and were settled at Paestum, to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they later married, provided only one wife per man, before the fifth day before the Ides of February, when Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus was consul for the third time and Marcus Cocceius Nerva was consul. For Centurion Liccaius Marsunnia, son of Birso, described and verified in the twenty-third place from the bronze tablet affixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the podium of the altar of the Julian gens. The names of the witnesses are: Marcus Virius Marcellus, decurion of the Savarian legion, Lucius Domitius Severus, a Breucian veteran, Gaius Marcius Nobilis, from Emona, Gaius Pidienus, from Aquileia, Lucius Valerius Pauo, a Breucian veteran, Gaius Julius Clarus, from Aquileia, Lucius Mineius Iucundus, from Aquileia.
EDCS-10200169.summary: This inscription is a military diploma from Emperor Vespasian, issued in 70 CE, granting Roman citizenship and the right of conubium (legal marriage) to veterans of the Misenum fleet, specifically identifying Centurion Liccaius Marsunnia as a recipient and listing the magistrates and witnesses.

EDCS-10200171.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Flavius Miles praefectus cohortis III Batavorum miliariae equitatae
EDCS-10200171.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Flavius Miles, prefect of the Third Cohort of Batavians, a thousand strong, mounted.
EDCS-10200171.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest by Marcus Flavius Miles, a military prefect, likely commemorating a vow or a building project.

EDCS-10701838.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et Silvano Domestico Caius Iulius Super votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10701838.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Silvanus Domesticus, Gaius Julius Super willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10701838.summary: This inscription records Gaius Julius Super's fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-10701840.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning the name Aelianus, likely part of a larger dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-10701842.translation: To Postumianus, [who lived] for [number] years, Quintus to his father.
EDCS-10701842.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Quintus to his father, Postumianus.

EDCS-11800553.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Septimia Ocna vixit nnos XXXV et Ulpiae Emeritae filiae eius vixit nnos XII et alio filio ipseus sic qui vixit menses VI Aelius Martinianus optio coniugi et filiis pientissimis titulum posuit
EDCS-11800553.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Septimia Ocna lived 35 years, and Ulpia Emerita, her daughter, lived 12 years, and another son of hers, who lived 6 months. Aelius Martinianus, an optio, placed this monument for his most devoted wife and sons.
EDCS-11800553.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Aelius Martinianus, an optio, to his wife Septimia Ocna and their two children, Ulpia Emerita and an unnamed son.

EDCS-11800554.text_edition: Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Respectus decurio municipii vivus fecit sibi et Ulpiae Amasiae coniugi Aelia Materio filia nnorum X hic sita est parentes titulum memoriae posuerunt
EDCS-11800554.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Respectus, decurion of the municipality, made this while living for himself and Ulpia Amasia, his wife. Aelia Materio, their daughter, 10 years old, is buried here; her parents placed this monument in her memory.
EDCS-11800554.summary: This inscription serves as a funerary monument made by Publius Aelius Respectus for himself, his wife Ulpia Amasia, and their deceased daughter Aelia Materio, a decurion of the municipality.

EDCS-11800555.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aelius...
EDCS-11800555.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest by an individual named Aelius.

EDCS-11800556.text_edition: P Nesergouna annorum LX hic sita est P Dubnomara filia et P3 Valerianus nepos? f(aciendum)? c(uraverunt)
EDCS-11800556.translation: P. Nesergouna, 60 years old, is buried here. P. Dubnomara, her daughter, and P. Valerianus, her grandson, saw to its making.
EDCS-11800556.summary: This is a funerary monument for P. Nesergouna, aged 60, erected by her daughter P. Dubnomara and her grandson P. Valerianus.

EDCS-11800602.text_edition: Lucius Beducius Claudia Secundus Viruno miles legionis II adiutricis
EDCS-11800602.translation: Lucius Beducius Claudia Secundus Viruno, a soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion.
EDCS-11800602.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Beducius Claudia Secundus Viruno as a soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion, likely from a tombstone or military dedication.

EDCS-11800604.text_edition: Aelius Saturninus veteranus legionis II adiutricis et Tullia Sperata coniux et Aelius Florianus et Aelius Verus filii Aelia Saturnina soror Septimius Quadratus pater vixit annis LXXX et Septimia Pusina mater vixit annis LXXX et Aeliae Florinae coniugi vixit annis XXV et Saturnae filiae vixit nnis IIII vivus sibi et suis faciendum curavit
EDCS-11800604.translation: Aelius Saturninus, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, and Tullia Sperata, his wife, and Aelius Florianus and Aelius Verus, his sons. Aelia Saturnina, his sister. Septimius Quadratus, his father, lived 80 years, and Septimia Pusina, his mother, lived 80 years. And to Aelia Florina, his wife, who lived 25 years. And to Saturnia, his daughter, who lived 4 years. He, while living, took care to have this made for himself and his family.
EDCS-11800604.summary: This is a funerary monument created by Aelius Saturninus, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, for himself and his entire family, including his wife, sons, sister, parents, and two deceased female relatives, Aelia Florina and Saturnia.

EDCS-11800605.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Prisciano quaestori tribuni legionis II adiutricis vixit annos XL dies XXIIII domo Mursa stipendiorum XXI Gaia ria coniux et et Priscilla et filias sic et
EDCS-11800605.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Priscianus, quaestor of the tribune of the Second Legion Adiutrix, who lived 40 years and 24 days, from Mursa, having served 21 years. Gaia Ria, his wife, and Priscilla, his daughter, thus [set this up].
EDCS-11800605.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Priscianus, a quaestor in the Second Legion Adiutrix, erected by his wife Gaia Ria and his daughter Priscilla, dating approximately to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800608.text_edition: Dis Manibus Herculanus vixit annos VIIII et Sterceia vixit annos XII et Revocata vixit annos III Aurelius Eutycies sic pater et Iulia Mamata filiis faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-11800608.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Herculanus lived 9 years, and Sterceia lived 12 years, and Revocata lived 3 years. Aurelius Eutyches, their father, and Iulia Mamata arranged for this to be made for their children.
EDCS-11800608.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Aurelius Eutyches and Iulia Mamata to their three young children, Herculanus, Sterceia, and Revocata, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800609.text_edition: Dis Manibus  et Paterno  matres quorum parvulis suis praecedunt in Orcum et Aurelio  Victorino iuniori vixit annos XVII  Aurelii Hermes et Bagia et Pitheus parentes filiis merentibus faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-11800609.translation: To the Divine Spirits and to Paternus, whose mothers precede their little ones to Orcus, and to Aurelius Victorinus the Younger, who lived 17 years. Aurelius Hermes and Bagia and Pitheus, parents, arranged for this to be made for their deserving children.
EDCS-11800609.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Paternus and Aurelius Victorinus the Younger, who died at 17, set up by their parents Aurelius Hermes, Bagia, and Pitheus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800640.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Victori quondam veterano legionis II adiutricis qui vixit nnos LV et Marco Ulpio Victorino quaestori coloniae Aquincensis et Ulpiae Victorinae quondam filiis eius Marcus Aurelius Marcellinus nepos et heres eius perfaciendum sic curavit
EDCS-11800640.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Marcus Ulpius Victor, formerly a veteran of the Second Legion Adiutrix, who lived 55 years, and to Marcus Ulpius Victorinus, quaestor of the colony of Aquincum, and to Ulpia Victorina, formerly his children. Marcus Aurelius Marcellinus, his grandson and heir, thus arranged for this to be completed.
EDCS-11800640.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Marcus Ulpius Victor, a veteran, and his two children, Marcus Ulpius Victorinus and Ulpia Victorina, erected by his grandson and heir Marcus Aurelius Marcellinus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800641.text_edition: Dis Manibus Septimio Messiano veterano ex beneficiario legati qui vixit nnos LX menses VII dies V Aurelia Vera marito dignissimo ob merita faciendum curavit
EDCS-11800641.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Septimius Messianus, a veteran from the staff of a legate, who lived 60 years, 7 months, and 5 days. Aurelia Vera arranged for this to be made for her most deserving husband, due to his merits.
EDCS-11800641.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Septimius Messianus, a veteran, commissioned by his wife Aurelia Vera, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800642.text_edition: Dis Manibus Lucius Varius Pudens veteranus legionis X geminae domo Parma annorum LX hic situs est Maria GEIS coniEugi#coniugi#CONEUGI pientissimo posuit
EDCS-11800642.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Varius Pudens, a veteran of the Tenth Legion Gemina, from Parma, 60 years old, is buried here. Maria Geis placed this for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-11800642.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Varius Pudens, a veteran from Parma, erected by his wife Maria Geis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800643.text_edition: Silvano Silvestro sic Septimius Karus miles legionis II adiutricis votum libens solvit
EDCS-11800643.translation: To Silvanus Silvester, Septimius Carus, a soldier of the Second Legion Adiutrix, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11800643.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the deity Silvanus Silvester by Septimius Carus, a soldier of the Second Legion Adiutrix, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800644.text_edition: Deo Soli Omnipotenti Iulius Marcellinus miles legionis II adiutricis ex voto posuit
EDCS-11800644.translation: To the Almighty God Sol, Iulius Marcellinus, a soldier of the Second Legion Adiutrix, placed this in accordance with a vow.
EDCS-11800644.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the God Sol Omnipotens by Iulius Marcellinus, a soldier of the Second Legion Adiutrix, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800645.text_edition: Valeria Cara Silvanabus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11800645.translation: Valeria Cara willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to the Silvanae.
EDCS-11800645.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Silvanae by Valeria Cara, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11800852.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Teutano the Preserver.
EDCS-11800852.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, specifically invoking him as Teutano the Preserver, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12000849.text_edition: Bono Evento renovavi ex voto pro salute ordinis Sirmiensium patronorum virorum emiEnentissimorum#eminentissimorum#EMENENTISSIMORUM Flavius Constantius custosR#custos#CUSTOR Matris deae libertus
EDCS-12000849.translation: To Good Event. Flavius Constantius, guardian of the Mother Goddess, a freedman, renewed (this) by vow for the well-being of the order of the Sirmians, their most eminent patrons, men.
EDCS-12000849.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bonus Eventus (Good Event) by Flavius Constantius, a freedman, on behalf of the Sirmians' patrons, likely a votive offering for prosperity or good fortune.

EDCS-12000850.text_edition: Dis Manibus Postumio Leoni viro egregio proximo liberto sacro Postumius Achilleus fratri benignissimo curante Crescentino amico suo et Dyscolio amico et coque liberto
EDCS-12000850.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Postumius Leo, an excellent man, the closest sacred freedman, Postumius Achilleus (erected this) for his most kindly brother, with the care of his friend Crescentinus and his friend and freedman cook Dyscolius.
EDCS-12000850.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Postumius Leo by his brother Postumius Achilleus, with the help of two friends, mourning an excellent and kind brother who was a freedman.

EDCS-12000853.text_edition: Aureliae Valentinae quae vixit nnos XXVII Marcellus eques numeri III Thracum coniugi faciendum curavit
EDCS-12000853.translation: To Aurelia Valentina, who lived 27 years. Marcellus, a cavalryman of the Third Thracian unit, took care to have this made for his wife.
EDCS-12000853.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aurelia Valentina, erected by her husband Marcellus, a cavalryman of the Third Thracian unit.

EDCS-12000856.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Calpurnius Ceius Aemilianus tribunus laticlavius legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-12000856.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Gaius Calpurnius Ceius Aemilianus, a laticlavian tribune of the Second Legion Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-12000856.summary: This inscription records Gaius Calpurnius Ceius Aemilianus, a military tribune, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely after a successful campaign or event.

EDCS-12000858.text_edition: Impperatores Caessares Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator III consul proconsul pater patriae et Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar filio sic Augusti nostri Dacici Germanici Sarmatici maximi ab Aquinco milia passuum CVII
EDCS-12000858.translation: The Emperors Caesars Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the second time, acclaimed imperator for the third time, consul, proconsul, father of the country, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, his son, thus of our Augustus, Dacian, Germanic, Sarmatian, greatest; 107 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-12000858.summary: This inscription is a mileage marker or dedicatory stone, likely from a road, dating to the reign of Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, specifying a distance from Aquincum.

EDCS-12100858.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100858.text_edition: Nero Claudius divi Claudi filius Germanici Caesaris nepos Tiberi Caesaris Augusti pronepos divi Augusti abnepos Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XI imperator VIIII pater patriae consul IIII peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohortibus tribus quae appellantur I et II Thracum et VII Breucorum et sunt in Germania sub Publio Sulpicio Scribonio Proculo qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XV Kalendas Iulias Aulo Licinio Nerva Siliano Publio Pasidieno Firmo consulibus pediti cohortis VII Breucorum cui praeest Caius Numisius Cai filius Velina Maximus Liccaio Liccai filio Breuco descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aeniaquae fixa est Romae in Capitolio ante aerarium militare in basi Claudiorum Marcellorum  Cai Marci Nobilis Emoniensis Sexti Tei Nicerotis Aquileiensis Cai Caecinae Hermaes sic Aquileiensis Titi Picati Carpi Aquileiensis Luci Hostili Blaesi Emoniensis Marci Treboni Hygini Aquileiensis Luci Anni Potentis Aquileiensis
EDCS-12100858.translation: Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the eleventh time, acclaimed imperator for the ninth time, father of the country, consul for the fourth time, granted citizenship to the infantrymen and cavalrymen who served in the three cohorts called I and II Thracum and VII Breucorum, who are in Germania under Publius Sulpicius Scribonius Proculus, who had completed twenty-five or more stipends, whose names are written below. He gave them and their children and descendants citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was given to them, or, if any were bachelors, with those whom they would marry thereafter, provided only one wife for each, before the 15th day before the Kalends of July, with Aulus Licinius Nerva Silianus and Publius Pasidienus Firmus as consuls. To Liccaius, son of Liccaius, a Breucus infantryman of the VII Breucorum cohort, commanded by Gaius Numisius Maximus, son of Gaius, of the Velina tribe. Transcribed and recognized from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the Capitol before the military treasury, on the base of the Claudii Marcelli. Witnesses: Gaius Marcius Nobilis of Emona, Sextus Teius Nicerotis of Aquileia, Gaius Caecina Hermaes of Aquileia, Titus Picatius Carpus of Aquileia, Lucius Hostilius Blaesus of Emona, Marcus Trebonius Hyginus of Aquileia, Lucius Annius Potens of Aquileia.
EDCS-12100858.summary: This is a military diploma issued by Emperor Nero in 65 AD, granting Roman citizenship and the right of marriage to veteran soldiers of three auxiliary cohorts serving in Germania, specifically recording the grant to Liccaius, a Breucian infantryman.

EDCS-12100879.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100879.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIX imperator II consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V quae appelTlantur#appellantur#APPETLANTUR I Thracum veterana sagittariorum et I cT(ivium)#c(ivium)#T Romanorum et I praetoria civium Romanorum et I Flavia Augusta Breti sic Mmiliaria et I Augusta Ituraeorum et cohortibus XIII I Alpinorum equitata et III Batavorum et I Thracum Germanica et I Alpinorum peditata et I Noricorum et III Lusitanorum et II Asturum et Callaecorum et VII Breucorum et I Lusitanorum et II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et I Cam=MApanorum#Campanorum#CMAP voluntariorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et sunt in Pannonia inferiore sub Iallio Basso legato quinque et viginti item classicis sex et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis hConesta#honesta#CONEST missiTone#missione#MISSTONE quorum nomina subscrepta sic sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent tum sic est civitas is dOata#data#OAT aMut#aut#MUT cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem VI Idus Februarias Marco Civica Barbaro Marco Metilio Regulo consulibus cohortis I Thracum Germanicae cui praeest Caius Turpilius Verecundus ex pedite Monno Tessimari filio Eravisco et Niciae Tricani filiae uxori eius Canac descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam  Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pantifex sic maximus tribunicia potestate XIX imperator II consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V quae appellantur I Thracum veterana sagittariorum et I civium Romanorum et I praetoria civium Romanorum et I Fl(avia) Augusta Britannica Mmiliaria et I Augusta Ituraeorum et cohortibus XIII I Alpinorum equitata et III Batavorum et I Thracum Germanica et I Alpinorum peditata et I Noricorum et III Lusitanorum et II Asturum et Callaecorum et VII Breucorum et I Lusitanorum et II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et I Campanorum voluntariorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et sunt in Pannonia inferiore sub Iallio Basso legato XXV item classicis XXVI stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent tum est civitas is data aut cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis  Cohortis I Thracum Germanicae cui praeest Caius Turpilius Verecundus ex pedite Monno Tessimari filio Eravisco et Niciae Tricani filiae uxori eius Canac  Marci Servili Getae Luci Pulli Chresimi Marci Sentili Iasi Tiberi Iuli Felicis Cai Belli Urbani Cai Pomponi Statiani Publi Ocili Prisci
EDCS-12100879.translation: The Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the nineteenth time, acclaimed imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, granted Roman citizenship to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in the five alae which are called I Thracum Veterana Sagittariorum, I Civium Romanorum, I Praetoria Civium Romanorum, I Flavia Augusta Britannica Miliaria, and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and in the thirteen cohorts: I Alpinorum Equitata, III Batavorum, I Thracum Germanica, I Alpinorum Pedita, I Noricorum, III Lusitanorum, II Asturum et Callaecorum, VII Breucorum, I Lusitanorum, II Augusta Thracum, I Montanorum, I Campanorum Voluntariorum, and I Thracum Civium Romanorum, and who are in Lower Pannonia under Iallius Bassus as legate, having honorably completed twenty-five stipends, likewise to the naval personnel having completed twenty-six stipends, who were honorably discharged, whose names are written below. He gave Roman citizenship to those who did not have it and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was given to them, or with those they would marry thereafter, provided only one wife for each, before the 6th day before the Ides of February, with Marcus Civica Barbarus and Marcus Metilius Regulus as consuls. For Monnus, son of Tessimarus, an Eraviscan infantryman of the I Thracum Germanica cohort, commanded by Gaius Turpilius Verecundus, and to Nicia, daughter of Tricani, his Eraviscan wife. Transcribed and recognized from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus near Minerva. (Repeat of the emperor's titulature and the grants, then names of witnesses for the record from the bronze tablet affixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus near Minerva: Marcus Servilius Geta, Lucius Pullius Chresimus, Marcus Sentilius Iasus, Tiberius Julius Felix, Gaius Bellius Urbanus, Gaius Pomponius Statianus, Publius Ocilius Priscus.)
EDCS-12100879.summary: This is a military diploma issued by Emperor Antoninus Pius in 156 AD, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to auxiliary veterans and naval personnel serving in Lower Pannonia, specifically for Monnus, an Eraviscan infantryman, and his wife Nicia.

EDCS-12100880.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100880.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIX imperator II consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V quae appellantur I Thracum veterana sagittariorum et I civium Romanorum et I praetoria civium Romanorum et I Flavia Augusta Britannica Mmiliaria et I Augusta Ituraeorum et cohortibus XIII I Alpinorum equitata et III Batavorum et I Thracum Germanica et I Alpinorum peditata et I Noricorum et III Lusitanorum et II Asturum et Callaecorum et VII Breucorum et I Lusitanorum et II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et I Campanorum voluntariorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et sunt in Pannonia inferiore sub Iallio Basso legato quinque et viginti item classicis sex et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas is data aut cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem VI Idus Februarias Marco Civica Barbaro Marco Metilio Regulo consulibus cohortis I Thracum Germanicae cui praeest Caius Turpilius Verecundus ex pedite Culso Atedumi filio Eravisco et Vervedae Tessimari filiae uxori eius Eraviscae descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam  Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIIII imperator II consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V quae appellantur I Thracum veterana sagittariorum et I civium Romanorum et I praetoria civium Romanorum et I Flavia Augusta Britannica Mmiliaria et I Augusta Ituraeorum et cohortibus XIII I Alpinorum equitata et III Batavorum et I Thracum Germanica et I Alpinorum peditata et I Noricorum et III Lusitanorum et II Asturum et Callaecorum et VII Breucorum et I Lusitanorum et II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et I Campanorum voluntariorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et sunt in Pannonia inferiore sub Iallio Basso legato XXV item classicis XXVI stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas is data aut cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis  ante diem VI Idus Februarias Marco Civica Barbaro et Regulo consulibus cohortis I Thracum Germanicae cui praeest Caius Turpilius Verecundus ex pedite Culso Atedumi filio Eravisco et Vervedae Tessimari filiae uxori eius Canac3 descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam  Marci Servili Getae Luci Pulli Chresimi Marci Sentili Iasi Tiberi Iuli Felicis Cai Belli Urbani Cai Pomponi Statiani Publi Ocili Prisci
EDCS-12100880.translation: The Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the nineteenth time, acclaimed imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, granted Roman citizenship to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in the five alae which are called I Thracum Veterana Sagittariorum, I Civium Romanorum, I Praetoria Civium Romanorum, I Flavia Augusta Britannica Miliaria, and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and in the thirteen cohorts: I Alpinorum Equitata, III Batavorum, I Thracum Germanica, I Alpinorum Pedita, I Noricorum, III Lusitanorum, II Asturum et Callaecorum, VII Breucorum, I Lusitanorum, II Augusta Thracum, I Montanorum, I Campanorum Voluntariorum, and I Thracum Civium Romanorum, and who are in Lower Pannonia under Iallius Bassus as legate, having honorably completed twenty-five stipends, likewise to the naval personnel having completed twenty-six stipends, who were honorably discharged, whose names are written below. He gave Roman citizenship to those who did not have it and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was given to them, or with those they would marry thereafter, provided only one wife for each, before the 6th day before the Ides of February, with Marcus Civica Barbarus and Marcus Metilius Regulus as consuls. For Culsus, son of Atedumus, an Eraviscan infantryman of the I Thracum Germanica cohort, commanded by Gaius Turpilius Verecundus, and to Verveda, daughter of Tessimarus, his Eraviscan wife. Transcribed and recognized from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus near Minerva. (Repeat of the emperor's titulature and the grants, then names of witnesses for the record from the bronze tablet affixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus near Minerva: Marcus Servilius Geta, Lucius Pullius Chresimus, Marcus Sentilius Iasus, Tiberius Julius Felix, Gaius Bellius Urbanus, Gaius Pomponius Statianus, Publius Ocilius Priscus.)
EDCS-12100880.summary: This is a military diploma issued by Emperor Antoninus Pius in 156 AD, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to auxiliary veterans and naval personnel serving in Lower Pannonia, specifically for Culsus, an Eraviscan infantryman, and his wife Verveda.

EDCS-12300207.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300207.text_edition: caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas Idibus Februariis cohortis II Hispanorum cui praeest Caius Cavarius Priscus equiti Dasenti Dasmeni filio Cornacati et Iorae Prososii filiae uxori eius et Emerito filio eius et Turunae filiae eius et Emeritae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio in aede Fidei populi Romani latere sinisteriore extrinsecus  Luci Vitelli Sossiani Quinti Vibi Saurici Titi Gratti Valentis Cai AntistiS#Antisti#ANTISTS Marini Publi Servili Adiutoris Auli Cascelli Successi Marci Heleni Primi
EDCS-12300207.translation: If they were bachelors, with those whom they would marry thereafter, provided only one wife for each, on the Ides of February. To Dasens, son of Dasmenus, a Cornacatian cavalryman of the II Hispanorum cohort, commanded by Gaius Cavarius Priscus, and to Iora, daughter of Prososius, his wife, and to his son Emeritus, and to his daughter Turuna, and to his daughter Emerita. Transcribed and recognized from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the Capitol in the Temple of Fides Populi Romani, on the left side outside. Witnesses: Lucius Vitellius Sossianus, Quintus Vibius Sauricus, Titus Grattius Valens, Gaius Antistius Marinus, Publius Servilius Adiutor, Aulus Cascellius Successus, Marcus Helenius Primus.
EDCS-12300207.summary: This inscription is part of a military diploma, likely from the mid-1st century AD, recording a grant of citizenship and marital rights to a cavalryman named Dasens, his wife Iora, and their children, specifying its official transcription from a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-12300222.text_edition: si peregrini iuris feminas matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant ac si ex duobus civibus Romanis natos ante diem III Kalendas Iunias Lucio Aelio Oculato Quinto Gavio Attico consulibus Lucio Flavio Luci filio Claudia Sabino Savariensi descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio  Cai Aconi Maximi Sisciensis Titi Flavi Festi Sisciensis Sexti Iuventi Ingenui Sirmiensis Cai Curti Secundi Sirmiensis Marci Statori Sabini Sirmiensis Marci Lucili Saturnini Sisciensis Marci Rutili Hermetis Sisciensis
EDCS-12300222.translation: If they marry foreign women, they may raise children as if born from two Roman citizens, before the 3rd day before the Kalends of June, with Lucius Aelius Oculatus and Quintus Gavius Atticus as consuls. To Lucius Flavius Sabinus, son of Lucius, of the Claudia tribe, from Savaria. Transcribed and recognized from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the Capitol. Witnesses: Gaius Aconius Maximus of Siscia, Titus Flavius Festus of Siscia, Sextus Iuventius Ingenuus of Sirmium, Gaius Curtius Secundus of Sirmium, Marcus Statorius Sabinus of Sirmium, Marcus Lucilius Saturninus of Siscia, Marcus Rutilius Hermes of Siscia.
EDCS-12300222.summary: This is part of a military diploma from 117 AD, confirming rights for veteran soldiers to legitimize children born to foreign wives, specifically mentioning Lucius Flavius Sabinus, a veteran from Savaria, and listing witnesses from Siscia and Sirmium.

EDCS-12300246.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300246.translation: Imperator Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, son of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul for the second time, granted citizenship to the foot soldiers and cavalrymen who are serving in two alae and five cohorts, which are named Siliana C.R., and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and I Montanorum C.R., and I Batavorum milliaria Pia Fidelis, and I Lusitanorum, and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and II Batavorum milliaria, and are in Pannonia under Gnaeus Pinarius Aemilius Cicatricula Pompeius Longinus, likewise to those honorably discharged, who have served twenty-five or more stipends, whose names are written below. He granted to them, their children, and their descendants citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted to them, or, if any were bachelors, with those they might marry later, provided each man married only one woman. Dated the 10th day before the Kalends of March, in the consulship of Imperator Caesar Traianus Augustus Germanicus for the second time and Sextus Iulius Frontinus for the second time. For the I Augusta Ituraeorum cohort, whose commander is Lucius Callidius Camidienus, son of Lucius, from the Stellatina tribe, to the honorably discharged foot soldier Publius Insteius Cyrrhus, son of Agrippa. Copied and verified from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of divine Augustus, near Minerva. [Witnesses:] Gaius Iunius Primus, Titus Flavius Secundus, Sextus Caesoni Callistus, Titus Flavius Abascantus, Quintus Pompeius Homerus, Lucius Valerius Basterna, Lucius Pullius Epaphroditus.
EDCS-12300246.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Trajan in 100 CE, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to auxiliary soldiers and their families serving in Pannonia, specifically detailing the discharge of Publius Insteius Cyrrhus from the Cohort I Augusta Ituraeorum.

EDCS-12300251.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300251.translation: Imperator Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, son of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the sixth time, acclaimed Imperator for the fourth time, father of the country, consul for the fourth time, designated consul for the fifth, granted to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers who are serving in three alae and five cohorts, which are named I Aravacorum, and I Flavia Britanniciana milliaria C.R., and Siliana C.R., and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and I Alpinorum, and I Montanorum, and II Alpinorum, and VIII Raetorum, and are in Pannonia under Quintus Glitius Atilius Agricola, who have served twenty-five or more stipends, likewise to those honorably discharged whose names are written below, citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted to them, or, if any were bachelors, with those they might marry later, provided each man married only one woman. Dated the 13th day before the Kalends of December, in the consulship of Lucius Antonius Albus and Marcus Iunius Homullus. For the I Montanorum cohort, whose commander is Quintus Caecilius Redditus, to the foot soldier Lucius Singeius Rufianus, son of Lucius, from Lucens. Copied and verified from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of divine Augustus, near Minerva.
EDCS-12300251.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Trajan in 103 CE, bestowing Roman citizenship and conubium upon auxiliary soldiers and their families serving in Pannonia, specifically for Lucius Singeius Rufianus of Cohort I Montanorum.

EDCS-12300304.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300304.translation: Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of divine Hadrian, grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the fifteenth time, acclaimed Imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, granted Roman citizenship to those who have served in the Praetorian Ravenna Fleet, which is under the prefect Tuticanius Capito, who have completed twenty-six stipends and been honorably discharged, whose names are written below. He granted to them, their children, and their descendants Roman citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted to them, or, if any were bachelors, with those they might marry later, provided each man married only one woman. Dated the Nones of September, in the consulship of Priscus and Romulus. For the ordinary seaman Gaius Valerius Dasius, son of Annaeus, a Scirtonian from Dalmatia. Copied and verified from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of divine Augustus, near Minerva. [Witnesses:] Marcus Servilius Geta, Lucius Pullius Chresimus, Marcus Sentilius Iasus, Tiberius Iulius Felix, Gaius Iulius Silvanus, Lucius Pullius Velox, Publius Ocilius Priscus.
EDCS-12300304.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Antoninus Pius in 152 CE, granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to sailors honorably discharged from the Praetorian Ravenna Fleet, specifically citing Gaius Valerius Dasius from Dalmatia.

EDCS-12300327.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300327.translation: Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the twenty-first time, acclaimed Imperator for the fifth time, consul for the third time, and Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Maximus, holding tribunician power for the seventh time, acclaimed Imperator for the fifth time, consul for the third time, fathers of the country, sons of divine Antoninus, grandsons of divine Hadrian, great-grandsons of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of divine Nerva, granted to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers who have served in three alae, named I Thracum veterana, and I Britannica milliaria C.R., and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and ten cohorts, named I Thracum Germanica C.R., and I Alpinorum peditata, and I Noricorum, and III Lusitanorum, and II Asturum et Callaecorum, and VII Breucorum, and I Lusitanorum, and II Augusta Thracum, and I Montanorum, and I Augusta Thracum, and are in Lower Pannonia under Claudius Pompeianus, legatus, who have completed twenty-five or more stipends and been honorably discharged, whose names are written below. They granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not possess it and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted to them, or with those they might marry later, provided each man married only one woman. Dated the 3rd day before the Nones of May, in the consulship of Dentilianus and Pallas. For the I Thracum veterana ala, whose commander is Flavius Macer, to the ordinary cavalryman Oxetius, son of Naevio, an Eraviscan. Copied and verified from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of divine Augustus, near Minerva. [Witnesses:] Tiberius Iulius Felix, Gaius Bellius Urbanus, Lucius Pullius Primus, Lucius Sentius Chrysogonus, Gaius Pomponius Statianus, Lucius Pullius Zosimus, Publius Ocilius Priscus.
EDCS-12300327.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by co-emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus in 167 CE, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to auxiliary soldiers and their families serving in Lower Pannonia, specifically for Oxetius, a cavalryman from Ala I Thracum veterana.

EDCS-12300346.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300346.translation: Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, son of divine Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of divine Severus Pius, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the fourth time, consul, father of the country, listed the names of the soldiers who served in the ten Praetorian Severian Pia Vindex cohorts, who have bravely and faithfully fulfilled their military service. He granted them the right of marriage, limited to single and first wives, so that even if they marry women of peregrine status, they may raise children as if born from two Roman citizens. Dated the 7th day before the Ides of January, in the consulship of Tiberius Manilius Fuscus for the second time and Servius Domitius Dexter. For the II Praetorian Severian Pia Vindex Cohort, to [---]tronius Martialis (?), son of Valerius, from the Papiria tribe, from Poetovio. Copied and verified from the bronze tablet which is affixed on the wall behind the temple of divine Augustus, near Minerva.
EDCS-12300346.summary: This is a fragment of a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Severus Alexander in 225 CE, granting marriage rights and citizenship to children of Praetorian Guard soldiers who have completed their service, specifically mentioning a soldier from the Second Praetorian Severian Pia Vindex Cohort named [---]tronius Martialis from Poetovio.

EDCS-12300383.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300383.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XI imperator sic et consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XIII quae appellantur I Flavia Britannica |(miliaria) et I Thracum veterana sagittaria et I Brittonum civium Romanorum et I praetoria civium Romanorum et I Augusta Ituraeorum et III Batavorum |(miliariae) vexillatio et I Alpinorum equitata et I Thracum Germanica et I Alpinorum peditata et I Noricorum et III Lusitanorum et II Nerviorum et Call=Haecorum#Callaecorum#CAHAEC et VII Breucorum et I Lusitanorum et II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et I Campanorum voluntariorum civium Romanorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et sunt in nnia inferiore sub Cominio Secundo quinis et vicenis <pluribusve> item classicis sex et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent item fi=EElis#filis#EELIS classicorum dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas is data aut cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem VII Idus Octobres Caio Fabio Arippino Marco Antonio Zenone consulibus alae I Flaviae Britannicae |(miliariae) cui praeest Marcus Licinius Victor Savaria ex sesquipliciario Fusco Luci filio Azalo descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam  Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XI imperator II consul IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XIII et sunt in Pannonia inferiore sub Cominio Secundo XXV pluribusve item classicis XXVI stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent item filis classicorum dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas data aut cum is quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis  ante diem VII Idus Octobres Agrippino et Zenone consulibus alae I Flaviae nnicae |(miliariae) cui praeest Marcus Licinius Victor Savaria ex sesquipliciario Fusco Luci filio Azalo  Luci Pulli Daphni Marci Servili Getae Luci Pulli Chresimi Marci Ulpi Blasti Tiberi Iuli Felicis Cai Iuli Silvani Publi Ocili Prisci
EDCS-12300383.translation: Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of divine Hadrian, grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the eleventh time, acclaimed Imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, granted Roman citizenship to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers who have served in five alae and thirteen cohorts, which are named I Flavia Britannica milliaria, and I Thracum veterana sagittaria, and I Brittonum civium Romanorum, and I Praetoria civium Romanorum, and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and the vexillation of III Batavorum milliaria, and I Alpinorum equitata, and I Thracum Germanica, and I Alpinorum peditata, and I Noricorum, and III Lusitanorum, and II Nerviorum et Callaecorum, and VII Breucorum, and I Lusitanorum, and II Augusta Thracum, and I Montanorum, and I Campanorum voluntariorum civium Romanorum, and I Thracum civium Romanorum, and are in Lower Pannonia under Cominius Secundus, who have completed twenty-five or more stipends, likewise to the naval personnel who have completed twenty-six stipends and been honorably discharged, whose names are written below. He granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not possess it, likewise to the children of the naval personnel, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted to them, or with those they might marry later, provided each man married only one woman. Dated the 7th day before the Ides of October, in the consulship of Gaius Fabius Agrippinus and Marcus Antonius Zeno. For the I Flavia Britannica milliaria ala, whose commander is Marcus Licinius Victor from Savaria, to the sesquiplicarius Fuscus, son of Lucius, an Azalus. Copied and verified from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of divine Augustus, near Minerva. [Witnesses:] Lucius Pullius Daphnus, Marcus Servilius Geta, Lucius Pullius Chresimus, Marcus Ulpius Blastus, Tiberius Iulius Felix, Gaius Iulius Silvanus, Publius Ocilius Priscus.
EDCS-12300383.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Antoninus Pius in 148 CE, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to auxiliary soldiers and naval personnel and their families serving in Lower Pannonia, specifically for Fuscus, a cavalryman from Ala I Flavia Britannica milliaria.

EDCS-12700093.text_edition: Marti Gradivo quem aput sic legionem VII Claudiam tempore quo ad eradendum nomen saevissimae dominationis missus cum vexillationes Moesiae inferioris voltus hhostium ppublicorum de vexillis et cantabris ultro detrahere nollent auctores improsperi conatus coercerat congressione facta prima in acie constitutus invocaverat Clodius Celsinus signum quod in bello voverat  posuit
EDCS-12700093.translation: To Mars Gradivus, whom Clodius Celsinus had invoked in the first battle line where he was stationed, when he was sent to erase the name of a most cruel domination and the vexillations of Lower Moesia were unwilling to spontaneously remove the faces of public enemies from their standards and cantabri, and he had restrained the authors of the unsuccessful attempt: he dedicated the sign which he had vowed in war.
EDCS-12700093.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Mars Gradivus by Clodius Celsinus during a military campaign to overthrow a tyrannical rule, likely dating to the Tetrarchic period, possibly around 310 CE, given the reference to 'erasing the name of a most cruel domination'.

EDCS-13302402.text_edition: Lucius Attius Macro legatus Augusti pro praetore cons(ul) designatus
EDCS-13302402.translation: Lucius Attius Macro, Augustan legate pro praetore, consul designate.
EDCS-13302402.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication or monument acknowledging Lucius Attius Macro in his capacity as a provincial governor (legate Augusti pro praetore) and consul designate, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302534.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Luci Corneli Latiniani legati Augusti pro praetore Imbrasus libertus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-13302534.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the well-being of Lucius Cornelius Latinianus, Augustan legate pro praetore, Imbrasus, his freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13302534.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, made by Imbrasus, a freedman, on behalf of his patron, Lucius Cornelius Latinianus, who held the position of an Augustan legate pro praetore, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13400042.text_edition: Aurelio Muciano veterano domo Thracia qui vixit nnis LXX mensibus X diebus IIII Aurelia Domnina coniux pientissimo faciendum curavit
EDCS-13400042.translation: Aurelia Domnina, his most devoted wife, arranged for this to be made for Aurelius Mucianus, a veteran from Thrace, who lived 70 years, 10 months, and 4 days.
EDCS-13400042.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated by Aurelia Domnina to her husband, Aurelius Mucianus, a veteran originating from Thrace, recording his age at death and the devotion of his wife.

EDCS-13400043.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Carus veteranus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Antoninianae pro salute sua suorumque omnium votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-13400043.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Publius Aelius Carus, a veteran of the Second Legion Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Antoniniana, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his own safety and that of all his kin.
EDCS-13400043.summary: This is a votive altar dedicated by a veteran of Legio II Adiutrix to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the well-being of himself and his family, likely from the early 3rd century AD due to the Antoniniana epithet.

EDCS-13400044.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo  lustro Aureli Pisonis primi pili legionis II adiutricis Aurelius Donatus mensor miles emeritus legionis  eiusdem votum solvit libens merito Severo et Quintiano consulibus
EDCS-13400044.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, during the lustrum of Aurelius Piso, primus pilus of the Second Legion Adiutrix, Aurelius Donatus, a surveyor and emeritus soldier of the same legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, in the consulship of Severus and Quintianus.
EDCS-13400044.summary: This votive inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by an emeritus military surveyor, dedicated during the lustrum of a primus pilus of Legio II Adiutrix, and precisely dated to 235 AD by the consuls Severus and Quintianus.

EDCS-13400045.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caius Aelius Alexander Fabia Ancora sic custos armorum legionis IIII Flaviae felicis stipendiorum XXII vixit nnos XLII Caius Iulius Frugi heres faciendum curavit
EDCS-13400045.translation: To the spirits of the dead. Gaius Aelius Alexander from the Fabia tribe, also known as Ancora, a custos armorum (keeper of arms) of the Fourth Legion Flavia Felix, served for 22 years and lived for 42 years. Gaius Iulius Frugi, his heir, had this monument made.
EDCS-13400045.summary: This is a funerary monument for Gaius Aelius Alexander, a keeper of arms in Legio IIII Flavia Felix, erected by his heir Gaius Iulius Frugi, providing details of his military service and age at death.

EDCS-13400046.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Valerius Proclianus veteranus qui vixit nnos LXX menses X dies X Aurelia Procla coniunx et Flavius Proclianus miles legionis I adiutricis filius una cum matre patri carissimo faciendum curavit
EDCS-13400046.translation: To the spirits of the dead. Valerius Proclianus, a veteran who lived 70 years, 10 months, and 10 days. Aurelia Procla, his wife, and Flavius Proclianus, a soldier of the First Legion Adiutrix, his son, together with his mother, took care to have this made for their dearest father.
EDCS-13400046.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valerius Proclianus, a veteran, erected by his wife Aurelia Procla and their son Flavius Proclianus, a soldier of Legio I Adiutrix, detailing the deceased's age.

EDCS-13400047.text_edition: --- ann(orum) V Marcus Saenius Nepotianus hastatus |(centurio) legionis II adiutricis et Flavia Festa parentes filio dulcissimo
EDCS-13400047.translation: [---] years old. Marcus Saenius Nepotianus, a hastatus centurion of the Second Legion Adiutrix, and Flavia Festa, his parents, [erected this] for their sweetest son.
EDCS-13400047.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records a monument erected by Marcus Saenius Nepotianus, a centurion of Legio II Adiutrix, and Flavia Festa for their beloved son, whose age is partially preserved as 5 years old.

EDCS-13400048.text_edition: us A primus pilus legionis  piae fidelis
EDCS-13400048.translation: [---]us A[---], primus pilus of the [---] Legion Pia Fidelis.
EDCS-13400048.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription identifying a primus pilus of a 'Pia Fidelis' legion, with the name and legion number largely lost.

EDCS-13400049.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et Laribus Militaribus Marcus Iallius Bassus legatus Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-13400049.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Military Lares, Marcus Iallius Bassus, imperial legate with pro-praetorian power.
EDCS-13400049.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Iallius Bassus, an imperial legate, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Military Lares, likely indicating his presence and authority in a province.

EDCS-13400050.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et dis deabusque Tiberius Haterius Saturninus legatus Auggustorum pro praetore sodalis Augustalis cum Latroniano filio tribuno militum
EDCS-13400050.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and to the gods and goddesses, Tiberius Haterius Saturninus, legate of the Augusti with pro-praetorian power, Augustal priest, with his son Latronianus, military tribune.
EDCS-13400050.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and other deities by Tiberius Haterius Saturninus, an imperial legate and Augustal priest, made jointly with his son Latronianus, a military tribune, suggesting a public or religious act during their joint presence in a province.

EDCS-13400051.text_edition: Iovi Iunoni Minervae Caius Valerius Sabinianus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus et Salviae Luciolae eius
EDCS-13400051.translation: To Jupiter, Juno, Minerva, Gaius Valerius Sabinianus, imperial legate with pro-praetorian power, consul designate, and to Salvia Luciola, his [wife].
EDCS-13400051.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Capitoline Triad (Jupiter, Juno, Minerva) by Gaius Valerius Sabinianus, an imperial legate and consul designate, alongside his wife Salvia Luciola, likely for the well-being or honor of their family or province.

EDCS-13400052.text_edition: Silvano Silvestri Gaius Titius Platanus Augustalis muncipii Aquincensium votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-13400052.translation: To Silvanus Silvester, Gaius Titius Platanus, an Augustalis of the municipium of Aquincum, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13400052.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus Silvester, dedicated by Gaius Titius Platanus, an Augustalis from the municipium of Aquincum, signifying a personal or communal religious observance.

EDCS-13600130.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo divi Marci Aureli divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti imperatori XI tribunicia potestate VI consuli II proconsuli Imperatori Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Luci Septimi Severo sic Pii Pertenacis sic Augusti nostri filio divi Antonini nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti et Publio Septimio Getae Caesari Augusti nostri filio curante Tiberio Claudio Claudidiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore ab Aquinco milia passuum XLIII
EDCS-13600130.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, brother of divine Marcus Aurelius and divine Commodus, grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of divine Nerva, Imperator for the 11th time, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Proconsul; To the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of our Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, grandson of divine Antoninus, great-grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus and divine Nerva; And to Publius Septimius Geta Caesar, son of our Augustus. Supervised by Tiberius Claudius Claudidianus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power. 43 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-13600130.summary: This milestone, erected by Tiberius Claudius Claudidianus, records the dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus, his son Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), and his younger son Geta, marking a distance 43 miles from Aquincum, likely dating to early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13600388.text_edition: Roma tibi subito motibus ibit amor  Rotas opera tenet arepo sator  cohortis
EDCS-13600388.translation: Rome, suddenly your love will go with motions. The sower Arepo holds the wheels with effort. Of the cohort.
EDCS-13600388.summary: This inscription presents the Sator Square palindrome, a Latin word square, possibly containing an early Christian hidden message, with an additional word 'cohortis'.

EDCS-13700284.text_edition: Nemesi Reginae Augustae  Aurelius Vindicianus cornicularius praefecti et Marcus Aurelius Vindex Iunior filius eius
